range.geographicRange: North America — breeds across Canada and United States; winter range extends through Mexico

elevationRange.note: Ubiquitous in lawns, parks, and forest edges at all elevations in Pike County

ecology.role: Omnivorous ground forager — controls insect populations and disperses fruit seeds; a visible indicator of lawn-to-forest edge ecology and spring phenology.

associatedSpecies.relationship: Nests in maple branches; feeds on soil invertebrates beneath maples

associatedSpecies.name: Earthworms
associatedSpecies.relationship: Primary prey on lawns — arrival timing linked to spring soil thaw

habitat.summary: Open ground near trees — lawns, parks, pastures, forest edges, and suburban gardens with nesting trees nearby.
preferred: Mowed lawns adjacent to deciduous trees
habitat.substrate: Short grass and bare soil for foraging; forked branches for mud-lined cup nests

phenology.seasonality: Among the first widespread spring migrants and breeders in the Northeast
phenology.activePeriod: Year-round in much of Pennsylvania; spring arrival often March–April; nesting April–July; fall flocks form August–October

regionalNotes.pike-county-pa: First robins on thawing lawns are a reliable late-winter phenology signal — earlier than leaf-out on maples.

identification.summary: Familiar orange-breasted thrush — larger than sparrows, runs and pauses on lawns, melodious caroling song at dawn.
fieldMarks: Dark head with white eye ring and broken white throat
Yellow bill (breeding) — can look darker in winter
White undertail coverts visible in flight
Runs upright on ground, stops abruptly to listen for prey

lookAlikes.name: Varied thrush (west only)
lookAlikes.distinction: Orange breast band and black necklace — not in eastern PA

lookAlikes.name: Eastern towhee
lookAlikes.distinction: White belly, rufous sides, black upperparts — different shape and behavior

considerations: Low risk — do not handle injured birds without wildlife rehabilitator guidance
Keep cats indoors during nesting season — major nest predator

observation: Never approach nests closely — photograph from distance with telephoto lens
Do not play recorded calls excessively during breeding season
Record song and behavior, not just checklist ticks
leaveNoTrace: Stay on trails near known nest trees
Do not remove eggs or feathers from nests — illegal and harmful
ethics.conservation: Robins are common but nest failure is high — observation should minimize stress during breeding.

tips: Low angle on lawn captures hunting pause behavior
Dawn chorus audio recording complements still images
Include nest tree context without revealing exact nest location publicly

education.overview: The American robin is many people's first bird — a gateway to phenology, migration timing, and the difference between a lawn and an ecosystem.
education.summary: Watch the pause-and-listen hunt on grass — that behavior is the lesson.
interestingFacts: Robins can produce three broods per season in favorable years
They detect prey by sight and by listening for soil movements
Spring flocks are not the same individuals that nested in your yard — migration mixes populations
fieldNotes: Date first robin on your street vs. first maple flower
Note whether birds are in flocks (fall/winter) or pairs (spring)
Listen at dawn — song perch is often the tallest tree on territory

futureObservations: First arrival date each spring at a fixed location
Nest attempt outcomes if monitored ethically from distance
Correlation between earthworm activity and robin foraging success
unknowns: Migration timing shifts with climate — long-term local records needed
Some winter robins may be residents vs. migrants — banding data required to confirm

media.alt: American robin pausing on a spring lawn
media.caption: Orange breast and upright foraging posture — placeholder

prompts: Habitat: lawn, edge, or interior forest?

research.notes: Cornell Lab eBird integration future — not connected in MVP
